name: JSON Formatter
intro: Format, beautify, and validate JSON instantly for APIs, logs, configs, and debugging.
description: Use this free JSON Formatter to format JSON online, beautify minified payloads, and make complex objects easier to read. It is useful for API responses, JSON logs, configuration files, webhooks, and frontend or backend debugging. Paste raw JSON to pretty print it, review nested structures, and catch invalid syntax before using the data elsewhere.
whyUse: Turn minified JSON into clean readable structured output
Inspect nested objects and arrays faster during debugging
Catch invalid JSON before sending data to an API or app
Review copied webhook payloads, logs, and config files more easily
Work in the browser without opening an editor or writing code
howToUse: Paste valid or raw JSON into the input box
Click Run Tool to format the payload
Review the formatted output with indentation and line breaks
If the input is invalid, fix the reported JSON issues and run it again
Copy the formatted result for debugging, documentation, or reuse

examples.explanation: Trailing commas are not valid in JSON and should be removed before formatting.

examples.title: Detect invalid JSON with unquoted keys
examples.explanation: JSON keys must use double quotes, unlike some JavaScript object literals.

commonErrors.issue: Trailing commas break JSON parsing
commonErrors.fix: Remove the last comma after the final key-value pair in an object or array.

commonErrors.issue: Object keys are not wrapped in double quotes
commonErrors.fix: Make sure every JSON key uses double quotes, for example "name" instead of name.

commonErrors.issue: Single quotes are used instead of double quotes
commonErrors.fix: Replace single quotes with double quotes for JSON keys and string values.

commonErrors.issue: Missing or mismatched brackets
commonErrors.fix: Check every opening brace and bracket to make sure it has a matching closing character.

commonErrors.issue: Only part of the JSON payload was pasted
commonErrors.fix: Copy the full object or array so the formatter can parse the complete structure.

useCases: Debugging REST API and GraphQL responses
Reading webhook payloads from Stripe, GitHub, or internal services
Checking application logs stored in JSON format
Cleaning configuration files before sharing them with a team
Reviewing exported JSON before converting it with related tools

faq.q: What does a JSON formatter do?
faq.a: A JSON formatter converts compact or messy JSON into a readable structure with indentation and line breaks.

faq.q: Is a JSON formatter the same as a JSON beautifier?
faq.a: Yes. JSON formatter and JSON beautifier usually mean the same thing: making JSON easier to read.

faq.q: Does this JSON formatter also validate JSON?
faq.a: Yes. If the input is not valid JSON, the tool returns an error instead of formatted output.

faq.q: What is the difference between JSON Formatter and JSON Validator?
faq.a: JSON Formatter focuses on readability, while JSON Validator focuses on checking whether the syntax is valid. This page does both, but the validator page is better if you only need a validity check.

faq.q: What is the difference between JSON Formatter and JSON Minifier?
faq.a: JSON Formatter adds whitespace and indentation for readability, while JSON Minifier removes whitespace to make the payload smaller.

faq.q: Why is my JSON invalid?
faq.a: The most common reasons are trailing commas, missing double quotes around keys, single quotes, broken brackets, or incomplete pasted data.

faq.q: Can I format nested JSON objects and arrays?
faq.a: Yes. Nested objects, arrays, and mixed structures are formatted into a much clearer layout.

faq.q: Can I use this tool for API debugging?
faq.a: Yes. It is especially useful for inspecting API responses, webhook payloads, and JSON logs.

faq.q: Is my data uploaded to a server?
faq.a: If your implementation runs in the browser only, the JSON stays on the client side. That makes it suitable for quick local formatting tasks.

faq.q: When should I use the JSON Validator page instead?
faq.a: Use the JSON Validator page when your main goal is to confirm syntax quickly without focusing on pretty output.

name: JSON Validator
intro: Validate JSON instantly and catch syntax errors before they break your app, API, or config.
description: Use this free JSON Validator to check whether your JSON is valid and structurally correct. It helps you catch syntax mistakes in API payloads, config files, webhook bodies, exported data, and copied JSON snippets before they cause errors in your code, tools, or integrations. Paste raw JSON to verify it quickly and identify common formatting mistakes such as trailing commas, missing quotes, broken brackets, or incomplete objects.
whyUse: Catch JSON syntax errors before sending data to an API or application
Verify copied payloads from logs, docs, browser tools, or webhooks
Check config files before deploying or sharing them with a team
Save time by spotting invalid structure before debugging the wrong thing
Validate JSON directly in the browser without installing an editor or extension
howToUse: Paste your JSON into the input box
Click Run Tool to validate the payload
Read the result to see whether the JSON is valid or invalid
If it fails, review the input for missing quotes, commas, or broken brackets
Fix the JSON and run the check again before using it elsewhere

commonErrors.issue: Trailing commas after the last item
commonErrors.fix: Remove the final comma in objects and arrays before validating again.

commonErrors.issue: Keys are written without double quotes
commonErrors.fix: Wrap every object key in double quotes, for example "name".

commonErrors.issue: Single quotes are used for strings
commonErrors.fix: Replace single quotes with valid JSON double quotes.

commonErrors.issue: Opening and closing brackets do not match
commonErrors.fix: Check that every { has a matching } and every [ has a matching ].

commonErrors.issue: Only part of the payload was pasted
commonErrors.fix: Copy the full JSON object or array instead of a truncated fragment.

useCases: Checking API request or response bodies before debugging
Validating JSON config files before deployment
Verifying webhook payloads during integration testing
Checking exported JSON from apps, CMS tools, or browser extensions
Confirming that copied JSON from logs or docs is safe to parse

faq.q: What does a JSON validator do?
faq.a: A JSON validator checks whether your JSON follows valid syntax rules and can be parsed correctly.

faq.q: What is the difference between JSON Validator and JSON Formatter?
faq.a: JSON Validator focuses on whether the JSON is valid. JSON Formatter focuses on making valid JSON easier to read.

faq.q: What is the difference between JSON Validator and JSON Minifier?
faq.a: JSON Validator checks syntax correctness. JSON Minifier removes whitespace from already valid JSON to make it smaller.

faq.q: Why is my JSON invalid?
faq.a: The usual reasons are trailing commas, missing double quotes around keys, single quotes, broken nesting, or incomplete copied data.

faq.q: Does valid JSON mean my data is correct?
faq.a: Not always. JSON can be syntactically valid but still contain the wrong values, field names, or schema for your application.

faq.q: Can I validate nested JSON objects and arrays?
faq.a: Yes. Deeply nested objects and arrays are supported as long as the structure is complete and valid.

faq.q: Can I use this to validate API payloads?
faq.a: Yes. This is one of the most common use cases for a JSON validator.

faq.q: Can I validate a config file with this tool?
faq.a: Yes. It is useful for checking JSON config files before deploying or sharing them.

faq.q: When should I use JSON Formatter after validation?
faq.a: Use JSON Formatter after validation when you want to inspect the structure visually or share the payload in a readable format.

faq.q: Is this useful for copied data from logs or browser dev tools?
faq.a: Yes. JSON copied from logs, network panels, and browser tools often includes mistakes or truncation, and this page is useful for catching that fast.

Paste valid JSON to minify it quickly without changing the underlying data structure.
whyUse: Reduce JSON size by removing indentation and line breaks
Prepare compact payloads for APIs, storage, or embedding
Clean up formatted JSON before using it in headers, configs, or scripts
Keep the same data structure while making the output smaller
Minify JSON directly in the browser without extra tools
howToUse: Paste valid JSON into the input box

Embedding JSON inside scripts, config values, or examples

Compressing readable JSON after inspection and debugging

faq.q: What does a JSON minifier do?
faq.a: A JSON minifier removes indentation, spaces, and line breaks from valid JSON to make it smaller and more compact.

faq.q: Does JSON minification change the data?
faq.a: No. It only removes unnecessary whitespace. The structure and values stay the same.

faq.q: What is the difference between JSON Minifier and JSON Formatter?
faq.a: JSON Minifier makes JSON smaller and compact. JSON Formatter makes JSON easier to read by adding indentation and line breaks.

faq.q: What is the difference between JSON Minifier and JSON Validator?
faq.a: JSON Minifier compresses valid JSON. JSON Validator checks whether the syntax is correct before you try to minify it.

faq.q: Why is my JSON not minifying?
faq.a: The most common reason is invalid JSON syntax, such as trailing commas, single quotes, missing quotes around keys, or incomplete pasted data.

faq.q: Can I minify nested JSON objects and arrays?
faq.a: Yes. Deeply nested objects and arrays are supported as long as the JSON is valid.

faq.q: Why would I minify JSON if storage is cheap?
faq.a: Smaller payloads can still be useful for transport, embedding, logging, testing, and reducing visual clutter in systems that expect compact input.

faq.q: Should I format JSON before minifying it?
faq.a: Formatting first can help you inspect and debug the payload. Once it is valid, you can minify it for compact output.

faq.q: Can I use this for API payloads and config data?
faq.a: Yes. Those are two of the most common use cases for a JSON minifier.

faq.q: When should I use JSON Formatter instead?
faq.a: Use JSON Formatter when you need readability, debugging, or visual inspection instead of compact output.

Check how a string looks after Base64 encoding before using it elsewhere
Work with encoded text directly in the browser during debugging

commonErrors.fix: Use URL Encoder for query strings and URLs, and Base64 Encoder only when a system expects Base64 data.

commonErrors.fix: Remember that Base64 is only encoding, not protection or secrecy.

commonErrors.fix: Clean the input text before encoding if exact output matters.

useCases: Creating Base64 strings for API testing and debugging
Preparing encoded values for headers or config fields
Encoding JSON or text before sending it to another system
Generating sample encoded data for docs, demos, or fixtures
Checking how Unicode or multiline text behaves after Base64 encoding

faq.q: What does a Base64 encoder do?
faq.a: A Base64 encoder converts text or data into a Base64 string format that can be transmitted or stored in text-based systems.

faq.q: Is Base64 the same as encryption?
faq.a: No. Base64 is encoding, not encryption. It makes data representable as text, but it does not secure it.

faq.q: What is the difference between Base64 Encoder and Base64 Decoder?
faq.a: Base64 Encoder converts plain text into Base64, while Base64 Decoder converts Base64 text back into readable text.

faq.q: When should I use Base64 encoding?
faq.a: Use Base64 when a tool, API, config, or protocol expects encoded text rather than raw plain text.

faq.q: Why does my Base64 output look different from another tool?
faq.a: Differences can happen because of character encoding, hidden spaces, line breaks, or URL-safe Base64 variants.

faq.q: Can I encode JSON with this tool?
faq.a: Yes. JSON text can be encoded as Base64 just like any other string.

faq.q: Can I encode Unicode or non-English text?
faq.a: Yes. Unicode text can be encoded, which is useful for testing international strings and payloads.

faq.q: Should I use this for URLs?
faq.a: Only if the target system specifically expects Base64. For regular links and query parameters, use URL Encoder instead.

faq.q: What is the difference between standard Base64 and Base64 URL?
faq.a: Standard Base64 uses characters like + and /, while Base64 URL uses URL-safe replacements such as - and _.

faq.q: When should I use Base64 Decoder after encoding?
faq.a: Use the decoder when you want to verify that the encoded value round-trips back to the original text correctly.

Prepare values for redirects, API requests, and tracking links
Encode text quickly without writing code or using browser consoles

Encoding raw text before pasting it into a request or script
Checking how Unicode, JSON, or special characters behave in a URL context

faq.q: What does a URL encoder do?
faq.a: A URL encoder converts unsafe or reserved characters into percent-encoded text so they can be used safely inside URLs and parameters.

faq.q: When should I use URL encoding?
faq.a: Use URL encoding when text contains spaces, symbols, Unicode characters, or reserved characters that must be transmitted safely in a URL.

faq.q: What is the difference between URL Encoder and URL Decoder?
faq.a: URL Encoder converts readable text into percent-encoded text, while URL Decoder turns encoded URL text back into readable form.

faq.q: What is the difference between URL encoding and Base64 encoding?
faq.a: URL encoding makes text safe for use inside URLs, while Base64 converts text into a different encoded format for data transport or storage.

faq.q: Should I encode a full URL or only a parameter value?
faq.a: That depends on the use case. Often only the parameter value should be encoded, but sometimes a full URL must be encoded when it is embedded inside another URL.

faq.q: Why does my URL look over-encoded?
faq.a: The most common reason is double-encoding, where a value that was already encoded gets encoded again.

faq.q: Can I encode Unicode or non-English text?
faq.a: Yes. URL encoding is useful for making international text safe inside query strings and other URL contexts.

faq.q: Can I encode JSON with this tool?
faq.a: Yes. If JSON text needs to be passed as a query value, it can be URL-encoded.

faq.q: Why are spaces encoded as %20?
faq.a: Spaces are not safe as raw characters in many URL contexts, so they are encoded into their percent-encoded representation.

faq.q: When should I use URL Decoder after encoding?
faq.a: Use URL Decoder when you want to reverse the encoded result or verify that the encoded value round-trips correctly.

Paste encoded text or a full encoded URL to decode it instantly in the browser.

Inspect query parameters, redirects, and tracking links more easily

Debug encoded request values without writing code
Reverse URL encoding directly in the browser during testing and troubleshooting

faq.q: What does a URL decoder do?
faq.a: A URL decoder converts percent-encoded URL text back into readable text so you can inspect its real contents.

faq.q: When should I use URL decoding?
faq.a: Use URL decoding when a link, query parameter, redirect target, or request value contains percent-encoded characters that you want to read clearly.

faq.q: What is the difference between URL Decoder and URL Encoder?
faq.a: URL Decoder turns percent-encoded text back into readable form, while URL Encoder converts readable text into a percent-encoded format.

faq.q: What is the difference between URL decoding and Base64 decoding?
faq.a: URL decoding reverses percent-encoding used in URLs, while Base64 decoding reverses Base64-encoded data. They solve different problems.

faq.q: Why does my URL look broken after decoding?
faq.a: The most common reasons are double-decoding, partial copied input, or decoding a value that was not supposed to be decoded as a whole.

faq.q: Can I decode a full embedded URL?
faq.a: Yes. This is a common use case when one URL is passed as a parameter inside another URL.

faq.q: Can I decode Unicode or non-English text?
faq.a: Yes. URL decoding is useful for restoring encoded Unicode characters back into readable text.

faq.q: Can I decode JSON with this tool?
faq.a: Yes. If JSON text was URL-encoded for transport in a query or redirect value, this tool can restore it.

faq.q: Why do I see %20, %2F, or %3D in encoded text?
faq.a: Those are percent-encoded representations of characters such as spaces, slashes, and equals signs.

faq.q: When should I use URL Encoder after decoding?
faq.a: Use URL Encoder when you want to re-encode the readable result for safe reuse inside a URL or query parameter.

name: HTML Encoder
intro: Encode special characters as HTML entities so they display safely in HTML content.
description: Use this HTML encoder to convert characters like <, >, &, and quotes into HTML entities. It is useful when displaying code snippets, protecting raw text inside markup, or preventing special characters from being interpreted as actual HTML.

faq.q: Why use HTML encoding?
faq.a: HTML encoding stops special characters from being treated as markup by the browser.

faq.q: What characters are usually encoded?
faq.a: Common examples include angle brackets, ampersands, and quotes.

faq.q: When is HTML encoding useful?
faq.a: It is useful for code snippets, content fields, templates, and any place where raw symbols should display as text.

faq.q: Is this HTML encoder free?
faq.a: Yes. It works online in the browser.

faq.q: What is the difference between HTML encoding and decoding?
faq.a: Encoding turns characters into HTML entities. Decoding turns HTML entities back into normal characters.

whyUse: Prevents special characters from being interpreted as HTML.
Useful for code snippets, templates, and pasted markup.
Helps display raw symbols safely in web content.
Good for editors, CMS fields, and content migration tasks.
Creates safe HTML entity output directly in the browser.
howToUse: Paste the raw text or markup into the input field.
Run the tool to convert special characters into HTML entities.
Review the encoded output.
Copy the result into your HTML, template, or content field.

examples.explanation: Encodes markup so it displays as text instead of being rendered as HTML.
Useful when quotes need to be safely represented in HTML.

useCases: Displaying code snippets safely on web pages.
Protecting special characters inside CMS or template fields.
Preparing raw markup text for documentation or content migration.

commonErrors.fix: Check whether your input already contains HTML entities before choosing the tool.

commonErrors.fix: Decide whether you want fully encoded output or want to preserve existing markup.

name: HTML Decoder
intro: Decode HTML entities back into normal readable characters and text.
description: Use this HTML decoder to convert entities like &, <, and " back into readable characters. It is useful when cleaning copied text, reviewing encoded markup, or converting stored HTML entities into normal readable output.

faq.q: What does an HTML decoder do?
faq.a: It converts HTML entities back into their original characters.

faq.q: Is this useful for copied HTML text?
faq.a: Yes. It helps when content contains entity codes instead of normal symbols.

faq.q: What kind of entities can it decode?

name: MD5 Hash Generator
intro: Generate an MD5 hash from text for quick checksums, comparisons, and test values.
description: Use this MD5 hash generator to create a deterministic MD5 value from any text input. It is useful for quick checksum checks, comparing strings, generating predictable test values, and understanding how the same input always produces the same MD5 output.

faq.q: What is an MD5 hash?
faq.a: MD5 is a hash function that converts input into a fixed-length value.

faq.q: Does the same text always produce the same MD5 hash?
faq.a: Yes. The same input will always generate the same MD5 output.

faq.q: Is MD5 secure for passwords?
faq.a: No. MD5 is outdated and should not be used for password storage.

faq.q: Is this MD5 hash generator free?
faq.a: Yes. You can use it online in the browser.

faq.q: What is the difference between MD5 and SHA256?

Creating quick checksums for copied text content.

faq.q: What does a DNS lookup show?
faq.a: A DNS lookup shows the technical records that help a domain resolve, such as A, AAAA, MX, TXT, NS, and CNAME when those records exist.

faq.q: What is the difference between DNS Lookup and WHOIS Lookup?
faq.a: DNS Lookup shows live DNS records, while WHOIS Lookup focuses on domain registration information such as registrar and expiration details.

faq.q: Can DNS Lookup help after changing hosting?
faq.a: Yes. It is useful for checking whether the current DNS records match the new hosting setup.

faq.q: Can I use this to check mail-related DNS records?
faq.a: Yes. A general DNS lookup may show MX and TXT records, though MX Record Lookup is better for focused mail routing checks.

faq.q: Why do DNS results sometimes differ between networks or tools?
faq.a: Caching, TTL values, resolver differences, and propagation timing can make DNS results vary.

faq.q: What is the difference between DNS Lookup and NS Record Lookup?
faq.a: DNS Lookup gives a broader DNS overview, while NS Record Lookup focuses specifically on nameserver delegation records.

faq.q: Why do I not see every record type for a domain?
faq.a: Not every domain uses every record type, so the result only reflects the records that actually exist.

faq.q: Can DNS Lookup help with TXT verification records?
faq.a: Yes. It is often useful for checking SPF, verification tokens, and other TXT-based records.

faq.q: Should I use DNS Lookup for mail delivery problems?
faq.a: It is a good starting point, but MX Record Lookup is often the better next step for mail routing issues.

faq.q: When should I use NS or MX lookup after DNS Lookup?
faq.a: Use record-specific tools when the issue is clearly related to nameserver delegation or mail routing rather than general DNS visibility.

name: Ping Test

faq.a: It removes repeated lines from line-based text and keeps only unique entries.

faq.q: What is the difference between Duplicate Line Remover and Remove Duplicate Words?
faq.a: Duplicate Line Remover works on full lines, while duplicate word tools work inside sentence-level text.

faq.a: Use a sorting tool after deduplication if you want the cleaned result in alphabetical or reverse order.

faq.q: What does a JWT decoder do?
faq.a: A JWT decoder reads the token header and payload so you can inspect the claims inside a JSON Web Token.

faq.q: Does decoding a JWT verify the signature?
faq.a: No. Decoding only shows the token content. Signature verification is a separate security check.

faq.q: What is the difference between JWT Decoder and Base64 Decoder?
faq.a: JWT Decoder is designed for dot-separated JWT tokens that use Base64 URL segments, while Base64 Decoder is for standard Base64 strings.

faq.q: Can I check JWT expiration with this tool?
faq.a: Yes. If the token includes an exp claim, you can inspect it after decoding.

faq.q: Why does my JWT fail to decode?
faq.a: Common reasons include missing token parts, broken Base64 URL data, copy errors, or a value that is not actually a JWT.

faq.q: Can I inspect roles, scopes, or permissions in a JWT?
faq.a: Yes. If those values are present in the payload, this tool can help you read them.

faq.q: What claims should I check first in a JWT?
faq.a: The most useful claims to check first are usually exp, iat, iss, aud, sub, and any app-specific roles or scopes.

faq.q: Is it safe to paste a JWT into a decoder?
faq.a: That depends on how the page is implemented. If decoding happens client-side in the browser, the token is not sent elsewhere during the decode step.

faq.q: Can I use this tool for access tokens and ID tokens?
faq.a: Yes. It is useful for both, as long as the token is in JWT format.

faq.q: Does a decoded JWT mean the user is authenticated correctly?
faq.a: Not by itself. A decoded token may still be expired, invalidly signed, issued for the wrong audience, or otherwise unusable.

Debug login failures caused by old or short-lived access tokens
Inspect exp and iat claims without manually decoding timestamps
Check refresh-token and session timing problems faster
Confirm whether auth issues are caused by token lifetime rather than permissions or roles

commonErrors.issue: Assuming the token is invalid only because it is expired
commonErrors.fix: Expired and invalid are not always the same. A token can be well-formed but no longer usable because of time-based claims.

commonErrors.issue: Checking exp without considering clock drift or environment time differences
commonErrors.fix: Compare token timestamps carefully and confirm system time is correct where the token is being used.

commonErrors.issue: Expecting the checker to verify signature trust automatically
commonErrors.fix: Expiry checking focuses on timing claims. Signature validation is a separate step.

commonErrors.issue: Using an incomplete token copied from logs or browser tools

commonErrors.issue: Confusing iat with exp
commonErrors.fix: iat usually shows when the token was issued, while exp shows when it should expire.

useCases: Debugging expired access tokens in API authentication flows
Checking why a session stopped working after some time
Inspecting token lifetime during OAuth or OpenID Connect testing
Confirming whether refresh logic is triggered too late
Ruling out expiry before investigating claims, roles, issuer, or audience issues

faq.q: What does a JWT expiry checker do?
faq.a: A JWT expiry checker reads token timing claims such as exp and helps you see whether the token appears expired or still valid.

faq.q: What is the difference between JWT Expiry Checker and JWT Decoder?
faq.a: JWT Expiry Checker focuses on time-based token status, while JWT Decoder gives a broader view of the header and payload.

faq.q: How can I tell if my JWT is expired?
faq.a: If the token includes an exp claim, the checker can compare that timestamp with the current time to determine whether it has expired.

faq.q: What does exp mean in a JWT?
faq.a: exp is the expiration timestamp that indicates when the token should no longer be accepted.

faq.q: What does iat mean in a JWT?
faq.a: iat usually stands for issued at and shows when the token was created.

faq.q: Why is my JWT failing even though it decodes correctly?
faq.a: A JWT can decode successfully and still fail because it is expired, issued for the wrong audience, or otherwise not valid for the current request.

faq.q: Can a JWT be valid structurally but still unusable?
faq.a: Yes. A token may parse and decode correctly but still be expired or fail signature or audience checks.

faq.q: What if the token has no exp claim?
faq.a: If there is no exp claim, the checker cannot determine expiration directly from the token payload.

faq.q: Does checking expiry verify the JWT signature?
faq.a: No. Expiry checking only looks at timing claims. Signature validation is separate.

faq.q: When should I use JWT Parser or JWT Decoder instead?
faq.a: Use JWT Parser or JWT Decoder when you want broader token inspection, not just expiration and timing details.

faq.q: What does a JWT parser do?
faq.a: A JWT parser breaks a token into readable sections and helps you inspect the claims inside the header and payload.

faq.q: What is the difference between JWT Parser and JWT Decoder?
faq.a: JWT Decoder focuses on quickly decoding token content, while JWT Parser emphasizes structured inspection of token parts and claims.

faq.q: Does parsing a JWT verify the signature?
faq.a: No. Parsing only reads the token content. Signature validation is a separate process.

faq.q: Can I inspect custom claims with this tool?
faq.a: Yes. Any custom claims stored in the payload can be inspected after parsing.

faq.q: Why does my JWT parser return an error?
faq.a: Common reasons include missing token parts, invalid JWT format, broken Base64 URL data, or copied input that is incomplete.

faq.q: Can I parse access tokens and ID tokens?
faq.a: Yes. If the token is a JWT, this tool can help inspect its structure and claims.

faq.q: Which claims are most useful to inspect first?
faq.a: The most useful claims often include exp, iat, iss, aud, sub, roles, scopes, and any custom fields relevant to your app.

faq.q: Can I compare two JWTs with a parser?
faq.a: Yes. Parsing makes it easier to compare the claims and structure of two tokens side by side.

faq.q: Does a parsed JWT mean the token is safe to trust?
faq.a: No. A parsed token may still be expired, invalidly signed, or otherwise untrusted.

faq.q: When should I use JWT Expiry Checker instead?
faq.a: Use JWT Expiry Checker when your main question is whether the token is expired or close to expiring.

faq.q: What does a Base64 decoder do?
faq.a: A Base64 decoder converts Base64-encoded text back into its original plain text or readable string form.

faq.q: What is the difference between Base64 Decoder and Base64 Encoder?
faq.a: Base64 Decoder turns encoded text back into readable text, while Base64 Encoder converts readable text into Base64.

faq.q: Why does decoding fail?
faq.a: The most common reasons are invalid characters, missing padding, truncated input, hidden whitespace, or using the wrong Base64 variant.

faq.q: Can I decode JSON with this tool?
faq.a: Yes. If a JSON string was encoded as Base64, this tool can decode it back into readable JSON text.

faq.q: Can I decode Unicode or non-English text?
faq.a: Yes. This is useful when checking encoded international text, symbols, or non-Latin strings.

faq.q: What is the difference between standard Base64 and Base64 URL?
faq.a: Standard Base64 uses characters like + and /, while Base64 URL uses URL-safe replacements such as - and _. They are similar but not always interchangeable.

faq.q: Why does my decoded result look strange?
faq.a: The encoded value may represent binary data, compressed data, or structured content instead of normal plain text.

faq.q: Can I use this tool to inspect API payloads and headers?
faq.a: Yes. That is one of the most practical uses for a Base64 decoder during development and debugging.

faq.q: Should I decode a JWT with this page?
faq.a: Not usually. JWT payloads use Base64 URL rather than standard Base64, so a JWT-specific tool is usually better.

faq.q: When should I use Base64 Encoder after decoding?
faq.a: Use the encoder when you want to confirm a round-trip conversion or recreate a valid Base64 value from readable text.

faq.a: It handles Base64URL input with - and _ characters and automatically restores missing padding when needed.

faq.q: What is this useful for?

faq.q: Why does decoding fail?
faq.a: Decoding usually fails when the pasted value is incomplete, has invalid characters, or belongs to a different encoding format.

faq.a: Yes. It works online in the browser.

commonErrors.fix: WHOIS usually shows registration-related information, not necessarily the DNS host or website server.

useCases: Checking registrar and renewal details before domain transfer or renewal work
Investigating domain ownership context during support or infrastructure changes
Reviewing nameserver information before troubleshooting DNS
Confirming whether a domain may be expired or close to expiration
Researching a domain before contacting the correct provider or registrar

faq.q: What does a WHOIS lookup show?
faq.a: A WHOIS lookup usually shows registration-related data such as registrar information, creation date, update date, expiration date, status, and nameservers when available.

faq.q: What is the difference between WHOIS Lookup and DNS Lookup?
faq.a: WHOIS Lookup focuses on domain registration information, while DNS Lookup focuses on technical DNS records such as A, MX, TXT, NS, and CNAME.

faq.q: Can WHOIS tell me who owns a domain?
faq.a: Sometimes, but not always. Many domains use privacy protection or registries that limit public owner details.

faq.q: Can I see when a domain expires?
faq.a: Often yes. Many WHOIS records include expiration dates, though the exact details depend on the registry and domain type.

faq.q: Why does WHOIS not show full contact information?
faq.a: Privacy protection, registry policies, and data protection rules often limit how much owner information is shown publicly.

faq.q: Can WHOIS show the nameservers for a domain?
faq.a: Yes. Many WHOIS records include current nameserver information, which can be useful for troubleshooting.

faq.q: Should I use WHOIS Lookup to troubleshoot email delivery?
faq.a: WHOIS can give domain context, but MX Record Lookup is usually the better tool for checking mail routing.

faq.q: Can WHOIS help before transferring a domain?
faq.a: Yes. It is useful for checking the current registrar, domain status, and expiration timing before transfer work.

faq.q: Why do some domains return very little WHOIS information?
faq.a: Some registries publish limited WHOIS data, and some domains are protected by privacy or registry restrictions.

faq.q: When should I use DNS or NS lookup after WHOIS?
faq.a: Use DNS or NS tools after WHOIS when you need technical resolution data rather than registration details.

name: MX Record Lookup
intro: Check MX records for a domain and see where its email is supposed to be delivered.
description: Use this free MX Record Lookup tool to check the mail exchange records for a domain and see which servers are configured to receive email for it. It is useful for troubleshooting email delivery problems, verifying mail provider setup, checking migration changes, reviewing priority values, and confirming whether a domain is pointed to the expected mail service before deeper SMTP or inbox troubleshooting.
whyUse: See which mail servers are configured to receive email for a domain
Check whether a domain points to the expected email provider
Verify MX changes after mail migration or DNS updates
Review priority values before troubleshooting email routing
Get a fast mail-routing check without using terminal tools
howToUse: Paste the domain name into the input box
Click Run Tool to fetch MX records
Review the returned mail servers and priority values
Compare the result with the mail provider you expected to see
If needed, follow up with DNS Lookup or TXT-related checks for broader mail troubleshooting

commonErrors.issue: Assuming MX records alone prove email will work correctly
commonErrors.fix: MX records are only one part of mail setup. TXT, SPF, DKIM, DMARC, mailbox provisioning, and provider-side settings may also matter.

commonErrors.issue: Ignoring MX priority values
commonErrors.fix: Check both the mail hostnames and their priority order to understand primary and backup routing.

commonErrors.issue: Expecting email changes to work immediately after updating DNS
commonErrors.fix: Allow time for propagation and caching, especially after migration or provider changes.

commonErrors.issue: Confusing MX records with website hosting records
commonErrors.fix: MX records control incoming mail routing, not website resolution.

commonErrors.issue: Stopping at MX when the real issue is SPF, DKIM, or mailbox-side configuration
commonErrors.fix: Use MX as a routing check, then continue with broader mail diagnostics if delivery still fails.

useCases: Checking whether a domain points to the correct mail provider
Verifying MX changes after email migration
Reviewing mail failover priorities for a domain
Troubleshooting email delivery setup before deeper SMTP investigation
Confirming whether a domain has any explicit mail routing records at all

faq.q: What does an MX record lookup show?
faq.a: An MX record lookup shows which mail servers are configured to receive email for a domain and what priority each one has.

faq.q: What is the difference between MX Record Lookup and DNS Lookup?
faq.a: MX Record Lookup focuses only on mail exchange records, while DNS Lookup provides a broader view of multiple DNS record types.

faq.q: Can I use this tool to check where a domain's email is hosted?
faq.a: Yes. MX records often reveal which mail service or provider is handling incoming email for the domain.

faq.q: Why are MX priority values important?
faq.a: Priority values help determine which mail server should be tried first and which ones act as backups.

faq.q: What does it mean if no MX records are found?
faq.a: It may mean the domain does not have explicit MX records configured, or the lookup failed because of DNS or domain issues.

faq.q: Can email still fail even if MX records look correct?
faq.a: Yes. Mail can still fail because of TXT-related policies, provider settings, mailbox issues, or broader DNS and routing problems.

faq.q: Should I use MX Record Lookup after moving email providers?
faq.a: Yes. It is one of the fastest ways to confirm whether the new mail routing is visible.

faq.q: What is the difference between MX records and A records?
faq.a: MX records control incoming mail routing, while A records map hostnames to IP addresses for general network resolution.

faq.q: Can I use this tool to troubleshoot Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace mail setup?
faq.a: Yes. It is useful for confirming that a domain points to the expected provider's mail servers.

faq.q: When should I use DNS Lookup after MX Record Lookup?
faq.a: Use DNS Lookup when you need a broader record overview, especially if the issue may involve TXT, NS, or other DNS records beyond MX.

faq.q: What does an NS record lookup show?
faq.a: An NS record lookup shows which nameservers are currently delegated for a domain.

faq.q: What is the difference between NS Record Lookup and DNS Lookup?
faq.a: NS Record Lookup focuses only on nameserver delegation, while DNS Lookup gives a broader view of multiple DNS record types.

faq.q: What is the difference between NS Record Lookup and WHOIS Lookup?
faq.a: NS Record Lookup is better for active delegated nameservers, while WHOIS Lookup focuses on registration and registrar-related information.

faq.q: Why are my nameservers different from what I set recently?
faq.a: The most common reasons are propagation delay, registry update timing, caching, or a registrar setting that was not fully applied.

faq.q: Can I use this tool after changing DNS providers?
faq.a: Yes. It is one of the best quick checks to confirm whether delegation moved to the new provider.

faq.q: Do NS records show mail routing or website IP addresses?
faq.a: No. NS records show nameserver delegation, not mail routing or A/AAAA host resolution.

faq.q: Can a domain work incorrectly even if NS records look right?
faq.a: Yes. The nameservers can be correct while other record types such as A, MX, or TXT are still wrong.

faq.q: Why does a domain sometimes return unexpected nameservers?
faq.a: It may still be delegated to an old provider, or you may be seeing propagation-related differences.

faq.q: Should I check NS records before checking A or MX records?
faq.a: Yes. If delegation itself is wrong, checking deeper record types may not explain the real problem.

faq.q: When should I use DNS Lookup after NS Record Lookup?
faq.a: Use DNS Lookup after confirming delegation when you need to inspect the actual DNS records hosted on those nameservers.

faq.q: Why would I check TXT records?

faq.a: Use TXT lookup for text-based DNS records like SPF or verification strings. Use MX lookup for mail-server routing records.

faq.q: What is this useful for?

faq.q: Why check an SOA record?

faq.q: What is this useful for?

faq.a: Use the expiry checker when you mainly want the remaining lifetime. Use the certificate checker when you need the full certificate details.

whyUse: Shows certificate expiration quickly without extra details.
Useful for monitoring, audits, and maintenance planning.
Helps catch certificates that are close to expiring.
Good for one-off domain health checks in the browser.
Runs directly in the browser with simple readable output.

Run the tool to check certificate expiry.

Copy the result if needed for notes or maintenance tracking.

Avoiding HTTPS downtime caused by expired certificates.

faq.a: Use TLS version checker when you want supported protocol information. Use the certificate checker when you want certificate metadata.

whyUse: Shows supported TLS protocol versions quickly.
Useful for audits, compatibility tests, and security reviews.
Helps confirm whether old protocols are still enabled.
Good for one-off HTTPS configuration checks in the browser.
Runs directly in the browser with readable protocol results.
howToUse: Paste the domain into the input box.
Run the tool to test supported TLS versions.
Review the protocol results.
Copy the output if needed for audits, notes, or remediation work.

examples.explanation: Checks which protocol versions the site appears to accept.
Shows modern TLS versions supported by a production domain.

useCases: Checking whether a site supports modern TLS versions.
Finding outdated TLS support during audits.
Reviewing HTTPS compatibility before deployment or policy updates.

commonErrors.issue: The user expects certificate expiry or issuer details instead of protocol support.

name: HSTS Checker
intro: Check whether a domain sends the Strict-Transport-Security header.
description: Use this HSTS checker to verify whether a domain returns the Strict-Transport-Security header and inspect key values such as max-age, includeSubDomains, and preload. It is useful for HTTPS hardening, security reviews, deployment checks, and header validation.
whyUse: Checks whether HSTS is enabled on a live domain.
Helps confirm that HTTPS hardening is configured correctly.
Useful for security reviews, migrations, and post-deployment validation.
Shows important HSTS values such as max-age and includeSubDomains.
Lets you verify the header quickly without opening developer tools.
howToUse: Enter a domain like example.com.
Run the checker to inspect the response headers.
Review whether the Strict-Transport-Security header is present.
Check max-age, includeSubDomains, and preload in the output.
Use the result to decide whether HSTS configuration needs changes.

examples.title: Domain with HSTS enabled
examples.output:

HSTS: Present
Header: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload
Max-Age: 31536000
Include Subdomains: Yes
Preload: Yes
examples.explanation: Shows a correctly returned HSTS header with common security directives.

examples.title: Domain without HSTS
examples.explanation: Useful when checking whether a site still needs HSTS to be enabled.

commonErrors.issue: A full URL with paths is pasted instead of a clean domain.
commonErrors.fix: Enter only the domain or hostname, such as example.com.

commonErrors.issue: The site redirects or blocks the request and no HSTS result is returned.
commonErrors.fix: Retry with the canonical HTTPS domain and confirm the site is reachable.

commonErrors.issue: The header exists only on HTTPS but the test uses the wrong target.
commonErrors.fix: Check the final secure hostname that serves the HTTPS response.

commonErrors.issue: The domain has HSTS but expected directives are missing.
commonErrors.fix: Review max-age, includeSubDomains, and preload separately in the output.

useCases: Checking whether HSTS is enabled after moving a site to HTTPS.
Validating security headers during a deployment review.
Confirming whether includeSubDomains is active for a main domain.
Reviewing whether a site is configured for stricter transport security.

faq.q: What is HSTS?
faq.a: HSTS stands for HTTP Strict Transport Security. It tells browsers to use HTTPS for future requests to the site.

faq.q: What does this checker look for?
faq.a: It checks whether the Strict-Transport-Security header is present and reads important directives such as max-age, includeSubDomains, and preload.

faq.q: What should I enter?
faq.a: Enter a domain or hostname like example.com.

faq.q: Why is HSTS useful?
faq.a: It helps reduce downgrade and insecure transport risks by forcing browsers to prefer HTTPS.

faq.q: What does includeSubDomains mean?
faq.a: It means the HSTS policy also applies to subdomains, not just the main hostname.

faq.q: What does preload mean?
faq.a: It signals that the domain may be intended for browser preload lists, though preload status itself is a separate process.

name: Robots.txt Checker
intro: Check whether a site has a robots.txt file and review its main directives.

whyUse: Quickly confirm whether robots.txt exists on a site.

faq.a: No. You can enter a domain like example.com or a full URL.

faq.a: No. It is a practical checker for existence and common directives, not a full crawler simulator.

faq.q: Why is robots.txt useful for SEO?
faq.a: It helps control crawler access and can reveal blocked sections or sitemap references.

faq.q: What if no robots.txt file is found?

faq.q: What does a canonical tag do?
faq.a: It tells search engines which URL is the preferred version of a page.

faq.q: Can a page have no canonical tag?
faq.a: Yes. The tool will show when no rel=canonical tag is found.

faq.q: Is a canonical tag the same as a redirect?
faq.a: No. A canonical is a page signal, while a redirect sends users and bots to a different URL.

faq.q: Should the canonical always match the input URL?
faq.a: Not always. Parameter pages or duplicates often point to a cleaner preferred version.

faq.q: Why is this useful for SEO?
faq.a: It helps reduce duplicate-content confusion and supports clearer indexing signals.

faq.a: Yes. You can test either a root domain or a specific page URL.

faq.q: What if the site is down?
faq.a: The tool will show an error or a non-success result so you can tell the site is not responding normally.

whyUse: Quickly resolve a hostname into IP addresses.
Useful for basic DNS and connectivity troubleshooting.
Shows IPv4 and IPv6 results separately.
Good for server checks and network study.
Works better for a site than traceroute-style tools.
howToUse: Paste a domain or hostname into the input box.
Run the tool to resolve DNS records.
Review the IPv4 and IPv6 addresses returned.
Use the result for troubleshooting or validation.

examples.explanation: Shows both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses when available.
Useful for quickly checking DNS resolution for a public hostname.

commonErrors.issue: A full URL is entered instead of a hostname.
commonErrors.fix: Use the domain or hostname only, or let the tool extract the host from a URL.

commonErrors.issue: The hostname does not exist.
commonErrors.fix: Check for typos and try again with the correct domain.

commonErrors.issue: Users expect ownership or geo data.
commonErrors.fix: This tool resolves IP addresses only. Use other tools for whois or IP info.

useCases: Checking whether a domain resolves correctly.
Comparing IPv4 and IPv6 availability.
Doing quick DNS troubleshooting before deeper checks.
Validating server hostnames during setup or support.

faq.q: What does this tool do?
faq.a: It resolves a hostname or domain into its IP addresses.

faq.q: Can it show both IPv4 and IPv6?
faq.a: Yes. It checks both address families separately when available.

faq.q: Can I paste a full URL?
faq.a: Yes. The tool can extract the hostname from a full URL.

faq.q: Why would no IP address be returned?
faq.a: The domain may not resolve, may be typed incorrectly, or may not have records for that address family.

faq.q: Is this the same as ping?
faq.a: No. This tool only resolves DNS to IP addresses and does not test reachability.

name: IP Range Calculator
intro: Calculate the usable IP range, network address, broadcast, and host count from CIDR input.
description: Use this free IP Range Calculator to find the network address, first usable IP, last usable IP, broadcast address, subnet mask, and total host capacity for an IPv4 subnet. It is useful for subnetting practice, network planning, VLAN design, firewall rules, access lists, lab work, and quick troubleshooting when you need to understand exactly which IP addresses belong to a subnet.
whyUse: See exactly which IP addresses belong to a subnet
Calculate first and last usable hosts without doing subnet math manually
Check network and broadcast addresses for firewall, routing, and VLAN work
Validate subnetting decisions during planning or troubleshooting
Practice CIDR and subnet calculations faster during study or lab work
howToUse: Paste an IPv4 address with CIDR notation such as 192.168.1.10/24 into the input box
Click Run Tool to calculate the subnet details
Review the network address, broadcast address, usable host range, and subnet mask
Use the result for planning, troubleshooting, documentation, or study
If needed, test another CIDR block to compare subnet sizes and ranges

examples.title: Typical /24 office subnet
examples.explanation: Useful for common LAN subnets where almost the entire last octet is available for hosts.

examples.title: Small /30 point-to-point subnet
examples.explanation: Helpful for understanding very small subnets often used on routed links.

examples.title: Larger /16 internal range
examples.explanation: Useful when checking large internal networks with many available hosts.

examples.title: Subnetted /27 branch network
examples.explanation: Helpful when designing smaller VLANs or branch office segments.

examples.title: Very large /8 network
examples.explanation: Useful for understanding how broad a large private range can be.

examples.title: Subnet for a /29 block
examples.explanation: Useful when working with small public IP allocations or lab ranges.

examples.explanation: The calculator rejects invalid octets or malformed CIDR values.

commonErrors.issue: Entering an IP without CIDR notation
commonErrors.fix: Include the prefix length, for example 192.168.1.10/24 instead of just 192.168.1.10.

commonErrors.issue: Confusing the network address with the first usable host
commonErrors.fix: Remember that the network address identifies the subnet itself and is not normally assigned to a host.

commonErrors.issue: Using the broadcast address as if it were a usable host
commonErrors.fix: The broadcast address is reserved for the subnet and is not normally assigned to a device.

commonErrors.issue: Assuming all subnet sizes have the same number of usable hosts
commonErrors.fix: Check the prefix length carefully because a /24, /27, and /30 have very different host capacity.

commonErrors.issue: Mixing host IP planning with ACL or route notation
commonErrors.fix: Decide whether you need a subnet range, a single host entry, or a summarized network before using the result.

useCases: Planning LAN and VLAN subnets during network design
Checking usable host ranges before assigning static IP addresses
Understanding network and broadcast boundaries for ACLs and firewall rules
Practicing subnetting for certification exams and lab work
Troubleshooting whether two IP addresses belong to the same subnet

faq.q: What does an IP range calculator do?
faq.a: An IP range calculator shows the network address, broadcast address, usable host range, and other subnet details based on an IPv4 address and CIDR prefix.

faq.q: What is the difference between network address and first usable IP?
faq.a: The network address identifies the subnet itself, while the first usable IP is the first address that can normally be assigned to a host.

faq.q: What is the difference between broadcast address and last usable IP?
faq.a: The broadcast address is reserved for the subnet, while the last usable IP is the final host address that can normally be assigned.

faq.q: Can I use this tool for subnetting practice?
faq.a: Yes. It is useful for learning CIDR, subnet masks, network boundaries, and host counts.

faq.q: Why does a /30 subnet only show a small usable range?
faq.a: A /30 subnet contains only four total addresses, and after reserving the network and broadcast addresses, only two are left for hosts.

faq.q: What happens with a /32 address?
faq.a: A /32 represents a single host address rather than a normal multi-host subnet.

faq.q: Can I use this for firewall and routing work?
faq.a: Yes. It is useful for understanding subnet boundaries when writing rules, routes, ACLs, and documentation.

faq.q: Does this tool work with private and public IPv4 ranges?
faq.a: Yes. The subnet math is the same whether the address belongs to a private or public range.

faq.q: Can this help me tell whether two hosts are in the same subnet?
faq.a: Yes. By checking the network boundaries, you can see whether two IP addresses belong to the same subnet.

faq.q: When should I use a CIDR calculator instead of doing the math manually?
faq.a: Use a calculator when you want a fast accurate answer during planning, support work, or exam practice without doing binary subnet math by hand.

name: My IP Address
intro: Find your current public IP address instantly for troubleshooting, allowlists, VPN checks, and network setup.
description: Use this free My IP Address tool to see your current public IP address instantly in the browser. It is useful for checking VPN or proxy changes, confirming which IP is visible to websites and APIs, adding your address to firewall or allowlist rules, troubleshooting remote access, and verifying network changes after reconnecting to a router, hotspot, or tunnel.
whyUse: See the public IP currently visible to websites and internet services
Check whether a VPN, proxy, or mobile hotspot changed your external IP
Copy your IP quickly for allowlists, firewall rules, or remote access setup
Confirm network changes after reconnecting, rebooting, or switching providers
Get the answer instantly without opening terminal commands or system settings
howToUse: Open the page without entering anything
Click Run Tool to detect your current public IP address
Review the result shown in the output box
Copy the IP if you need it for a firewall, allowlist, tunnel, or support task
Run it again after changing VPN, Wi-Fi, proxy, or mobile network to compare results

examples.title: Check your current home IP
examples.explanation: Useful when you need the IP currently exposed by your home internet connection.

examples.title: Check whether a VPN changed your IP
examples.explanation: Helpful for confirming that a VPN or proxy is changing your visible public IP.

examples.title: Copy IP for allowlisting
examples.explanation: Useful when you need to provide your IP to a support team or add it to an allowlist.

examples.title: Check mobile hotspot IP
examples.explanation: Useful when switching from home Wi-Fi to mobile data or hotspot connections.

examples.title: Verify IP after reconnecting router
examples.explanation: Helpful when checking whether reconnecting changed the WAN-facing address.

examples.title: Check IP before remote access setup
examples.explanation: Useful when preparing firewall, SSH, RDP, or tunnel rules that need your public IP.

examples.title: Check if office network exposes a different IP
examples.explanation: Helpful when comparing home, office, and VPN network egress addresses.

examples.title: Temporary detection failure
examples.output: Unable to detect your IP address.
examples.explanation: This can happen if the lookup request fails, the service is blocked, or there is a temporary connectivity issue.

commonErrors.issue: Confusing public IP with a local/private IP like 192.168.x.x or 10.x.x.x
commonErrors.fix: Remember that this tool shows your public internet-facing IP, not the private address assigned inside your local network.

commonErrors.issue: Assuming the visible IP belongs to one device only
commonErrors.fix: Many devices on the same network may share the same public IP through NAT.

commonErrors.issue: Expecting the IP to stay fixed permanently
commonErrors.fix: Many internet providers assign dynamic public IPs that can change after reconnecting or over time.

commonErrors.issue: Thinking a VPN is active even though the IP did not change
commonErrors.fix: Run the check before and after enabling the VPN to confirm whether the public IP actually changed.

commonErrors.issue: Using the current IP in rules without checking whether it may change later
commonErrors.fix: If the IP is dynamic, be prepared to update allowlists, firewall rules, or remote access settings when it changes.

useCases: Checking the current public IP before configuring remote access
Confirming whether a VPN or proxy changed the visible internet IP
Providing your IP to a support engineer or firewall administrator
Testing whether reconnecting a router changed the WAN-facing address
Comparing public IPs across home, office, hotspot, and tunnel connections

faq.q: What does My IP Address show?
faq.a: It shows the public IP address currently visible to websites and internet services.

faq.q: What is the difference between a public IP and a private IP?
faq.a: A public IP is the address seen on the internet, while a private IP is used only inside your local network, such as 192.168.x.x or 10.x.x.x.

faq.q: Why is my IP different from the one shown in my router or device settings?
faq.a: Your device may have a private local IP, while this tool shows the public IP used by your network to reach the internet.

faq.q: Can multiple devices have the same public IP?
faq.a: Yes. Devices behind the same router often share one public IP through network address translation.

faq.q: Why did my public IP change?
faq.a: It may change because of a dynamic ISP assignment, reconnecting your router, switching networks, or enabling a VPN or proxy.

faq.q: Can I use this tool to check whether my VPN is working?
faq.a: Yes. Compare the result before and after enabling the VPN to see whether your visible public IP changed.

faq.q: Is this my IPv4 address or IPv6 address?
faq.a: That depends on how the detection is implemented, but many simple checkers return the current public IPv4 address.

faq.q: Can I use this IP in a firewall or allowlist?
faq.a: Yes. That is one of the most common reasons to check your public IP, especially for temporary access rules.

faq.q: Why does the tool fail to detect my IP sometimes?
faq.a: Temporary network issues, blocked lookup services, browser restrictions, or connectivity problems can cause detection to fail.

faq.q: When should I use IP Range Calculator instead?
faq.a: Use IP Range Calculator when you need subnet math and host range planning. Use My IP Address when you only need the public IP currently visible on the internet.

useCases: Checking IPv4 broadcast addresses during subnetting practice.

Quickly calculating broadcast values for network planning.

faq.q: What does a broadcast address do?
faq.a: It identifies the last address in a standard IPv4 subnet and is used to send traffic to all hosts in that subnet.

faq.q: What input format should I use?
faq.a: Use IPv4 with CIDR notation, such as 192.168.1.10/24.

faq.q: Does this support IPv6?

faq.q: Why is this useful?
faq.a: It helps validate subnet boundaries and supports subnetting practice and configuration work.

faq.q: Is the broadcast address always the last address in the subnet?

whyUse: Quickly classifies a port into its standard range.
Useful for networking study and exam prep.
Helps review firewall or service port choices.
Simple way to understand port categories.

commonErrors.fix: This tool classifies the port range only and does not test whether the port is open.

useCases: Studying networking and port classifications.
Checking whether a service port is system, registered, or dynamic.
Reviewing firewall rules during documentation work.
Doing quick port reference checks during troubleshooting.

faq.q: What does this tool check?
faq.a: It checks which standard port range a port number belongs to.

faq.q: Does it scan whether the port is open?
faq.a: No. It classifies the number only and does not test live connectivity.

faq.q: What are well-known ports?
faq.a: They are ports from 0 to 1023 typically reserved for common system services.

faq.q: What are registered ports?
faq.a: They are ports from 1024 to 49151 often used by user applications and vendor services.

faq.q: What are dynamic or private ports?
faq.a: They are ports from 49152 to 65535 commonly used for ephemeral client-side connections.

faq.q: Does it include fees or insurance?
faq.a: No. It calculates EMI from loan amount, rate, and term only.

faq.q: How is it different from Mortgage Calculator?
faq.a: EMI Calculator is framed for general loans and uses loan term in months.

name: APR Calculator
intro: Calculate APR from loan amount, fees, interest rate, and term.
description: Use this APR calculator to estimate the annual percentage rate of a loan based on principal, annual interest rate, upfront fees, and loan term. It is useful for comparing loan offers more accurately than rate alone.
whyUse: Helps compare loans using a broader cost view.
Useful when rates alone do not show total borrowing cost.
Includes fees in the estimated annual percentage rate.
Fast for browser-based lending comparisons.
howToUse: Enter loan amount on the first line.
Enter annual interest rate on the second line.
Enter upfront fees on the third line.
Enter loan term in years on the fourth line, then run the tool.

examples.title: Loan with fees
examples.output:
Loan amount: 100000.00
Annual interest rate: 8.00%
Fees: 2000.00
Term: 5.00 years
Estimated APR: 8.40%

examples.title: Lower fee loan
examples.output:

Annual interest rate: 6.50%
Fees: 500.00
Term: 3.00 years

commonErrors.issue: Entering rate as 0.08 instead of 8.
commonErrors.fix: Enter annual interest rate as a percentage like 8 for 8%.

commonErrors.issue: Leaving out fees when the loan has upfront costs.
commonErrors.fix: Include all upfront fees to get a more realistic APR estimate.

commonErrors.issue: Expecting a lender-grade legal APR disclosure.
commonErrors.fix: This tool gives a simplified APR estimate for comparison purposes.

useCases: Comparing loan offers with different fee structures.
Checking the real cost of borrowing.
Reviewing loan offers before applying.
Estimating how fees affect annual borrowing cost.

faq.q: What does APR Calculator do?
faq.a: It estimates annual percentage rate by combining interest rate and annualized fees.

faq.q: How is APR different from interest rate?
faq.a: APR includes more of the total borrowing cost, not just the nominal interest rate.

faq.q: Does it use a simplified formula?
faq.a: Yes. This version estimates APR for comparison and planning, not formal lending disclosure.

faq.q: How do I enter the term?
faq.a: Enter the loan term in years.

faq.q: Can I use decimal rates and fees?
faq.a: Yes. All numeric inputs can include decimals.

name: Hourly to Salary Calculator
intro: Convert hourly pay into weekly, monthly, and annual salary estimates.
description: Use this hourly to salary calculator to convert an hourly wage into weekly, monthly, and yearly pay based on hours worked per week and weeks worked per year.
whyUse: Shows weekly, monthly, and yearly earnings from hourly pay.
Useful for job comparisons and pay planning.
Lets you adjust hours per week and working weeks per year.
Fast for browser-based pay conversion.
howToUse: Enter hourly rate on the first line.
Enter hours worked per week on the second line.
Enter weeks worked per year on the third line.
Run the tool to calculate salary estimates.

commonErrors.issue: Using monthly hours instead of weekly hours.
commonErrors.fix: Enter hours worked per week on the second line.

commonErrors.issue: Forgetting unpaid weeks or vacation time.
commonErrors.fix: Adjust weeks worked per year to match your real schedule.

commonErrors.issue: Expecting tax deductions in the result.
commonErrors.fix: This tool calculates gross pay estimates only.

useCases: Comparing hourly job offers.
Estimating annual income from hourly work.
Planning work schedules and income targets.
Checking the effect of reduced or extra weekly hours.

faq.q: What does Hourly to Salary Calculator do?
faq.a: It converts hourly pay into weekly, monthly, and annual salary estimates.

faq.q: How do I enter working time?
faq.a: Enter hours per week and weeks worked per year.

faq.q: Does it include taxes?
faq.a: No. It calculates gross pay only.

faq.q: Can I use part-time hours?
faq.a: Yes. Any valid hours-per-week value can be used.

faq.q: How is monthly pay calculated?
faq.a: Monthly pay is estimated as annual salary divided by 12.

Fast for browser-based pay conversion.

Run the tool to calculate hourly and other pay estimates.

commonErrors.issue: Using monthly salary instead of annual salary.
commonErrors.fix: Enter the yearly gross salary on the first line.

faq.q: What does Salary to Hourly Calculator do?
faq.a: It converts annual salary into hourly, weekly, and monthly gross pay estimates.

faq.q: How do I enter the schedule?
faq.a: Enter hours worked per week and weeks worked per year.

faq.q: Does it include taxes?
faq.a: No. It calculates gross pay only.

faq.q: Can I use non-standard schedules?
faq.a: Yes. Any valid work schedule assumptions can be entered.

faq.q: How is hourly rate calculated?
faq.a: Hourly rate is annual salary divided by total hours worked per year.
